query: effectiveness of apremilast in treating oral ulcers associated with Behçet’s syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Behcet Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Prevention of recurrent oral and genital ulcers:
retrieved_docs.content: Colchicine 1 to 2 mg/day in divided doses First-line drug Doses in the range of 1.2 to 1.8 mg can be used when 0.5 mg tablets are unavailable. [29] More effective in genital ulcers. Has a narrow therapeutic index. Cytopenias are a significant side effect. Apremilast is titrated at a rate of 10 mg daily over six days to attain a maintenance dose of 30 mg twice daily. [30] More effective in oral ulcers. Adverse events include nausea, diarrhea, and headache.

retrieved_docs.title: Apremilast -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Apremilast is classified as a small-molecule phosphodiesterase 4 (PDE4) inhibitor. It is indicated for psoriatic arthritis in adult patients with moderately to severely active disease, plaque psoriasis in adult patients that are candidates for phototherapy or systemic therapy, and adults with oral ulcers associated with Behcet's disease. Managing patients with such ailments necessitates interprofessional communication and continuity of care from the healthcare team. The interprofessional healthcare team should include a primary care physician (PCP), a rheumatologist, a dermatologist, a physical therapist (PT), nursing staff, and a pharmacist. Open-ended communication among patients and providers and thorough clinical analysis by the healthcare team can lead to more precise and practical treatment strategies leading to improved patient outcomes. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Apremilast -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Apremilast is FDA approved for treating psoriatic arthritis in adult patients with moderately to severely active disease, plaque psoriasis in adult patients who are candidates for phototherapy or systemic therapy, and adults with Behcet disease associated with oral ulcers. It is a small molecule phosphodiesterase 4 (PDE4) inhibitor. Apremilast is also used off-label for various dermatologic disorders that are unresponsive or ineffective to conventional therapy (systemic corticosteroids or immunosuppressive agents). This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors pertinent to interprofessional team members in managing patients with moderate to severe psoriatic arthritis unresponsive to first-line therapy, plaque psoriasis, and Behcet disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Apremilast -- Indications -- FDA Approved Use
retrieved_docs.content: Psoriatic arthritis Plaque psoriasis Oral ulcers associated with Behcet disease

retrieved_docs.title: Apremilast -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of apremilast. Describe the potential adverse effects of apremilast. Review the appropriate monitoring for patients receiving apremilast. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance apremilast use in treating psoriatic arthritis, plaque psoriasis, and Behcet disease and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Apremilast -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The interprofessional team should also regularly review the latest guidelines on psoriatic arthritis, plaque psoriasis, and oral ulcers associated with Behcet disease. Patients with any known hypersensitivity to apremilast and its constituents are contraindicated for treatment. Apremilast is labeled as pregnancy category C. Women of reproductive potential should be advised about the potential risk of pregnancy loss with apremilast. They should be informed of pregnancy planning and prevention. Contraception should be considered prior to initiate apremilast treatment in childbearing females. Lactating and breastfeeding women should also be advised not to receive apremilast. The primary care physician should be aware of the patient's vaccination status, and all immunizations should be up to date before starting treatment. The patient should also be advised that receiving live vaccinations during treatment with apremilast is not recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Apremilast -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Apremilast is classified as a small-molecule phosphodiesterase 4 (PDE4) inhibitor. [1] It is FDA approved for plaque psoriasis in adult patients that are candidates for phototherapy or systemic therapy and psoriatic arthritis in adult patients with moderately to severely active disease. [2] It is the first and only FDA-approved oral medication for oral ulcers associated with Behcet disease. [3] Apremilast is also used off-label for various dermatologic diseases that are unresponsive or ineffective to conventional therapy (systemic corticosteroids or immunosuppressive agents). Dermatologic diseases include hidradenitis suppurativa (acne inversa), atopic dermatitis (eczema), and alopecia areata. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Behcet Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Isolated oral and genital aphthae:
retrieved_docs.content: Initial treatment with topical triamcinolone acetonide cream 3 to 4 times a day until the pain from the ulcer is relieved [27] Topical sucralfate 1g/5mL four times daily with or as an alternative to topical corticosteroids [28] Topical anesthetics, generally not as effective as steroids or sucralfate, can also provide temporary relief

retrieved_docs.title: Behcet Disease -- History and Physical -- Aphthae
retrieved_docs.content: Oral ulcers occur in 97% to 99% of patients with Behcet disease, often representing the initial clinical feature. Lesions are usually painful, recurrent, and multiple and may involve the soft palate, hard palate, buccal mucosa, tongue, gingiva, lips, and tonsils. More than 90% of oral ulcers heal without scarring.

retrieved_docs.title: Behcet Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Gastrointestinal ulceration:
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids plus azathioprine Starting dose of prednisone 0.5 to 1 mg/kg daily [42] Given for at least one month or until the resolution of symptoms improve Decrease the daily dose to 10 mg/day over two to three months. Azathioprine started at the same time as prednisone The initial dose of azathioprine is 50 mg daily. As tolerated, increase the daily dose by 50 mg every four weeks. The target dose is 2.5 mg/kg/day. Complete blood count (CBC) is monitored every two weeks initially till the maximum required dose is achieved and then every 6 to 12 weeks. TNF-alpha inhibitors [43] Used when patients do not respond to glucocorticoids plus azathioprine Infliximab and adalimumab have been used successfully.

Outline the management options available for Behcet disease. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care and outcomes in patients with Behcet disease. retrieved_docs.title: Behcet Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Multiple lesions or isolated oral aphthae or genital ulcers are refractory to the therapy mentioned above:
retrieved_docs.content: Prednisone 15 mg/day, tapering to 10 mg/day after one week and completing the course over a two- to three-week period Low-dose prednisone (e.g., 5 mg/day) with a prolonged course may be used in case of recurrent oral aphthae

retrieved_docs.title: Behcet Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Behcet disease was first described in 1937 by Hulusi Behçet from Istanbul, who described three patients with oral and genital ulcerations, uveitis, and erythema nodosum. Other clinical features were identified later and were added to the disease spectrum. Behcet disease is an auto-inflammatory systemic vasculitis of unknown etiology. It is characterized by mucocutaneous manifestations, including recurrent oral and genital ulcerations, ocular manifestations, especially chronic relapsing uveitis, and systemic vasculitis involving arteries and veins of all sizes. It is also known as Behcet syndrome and malignant aphthosis. This activity describes the pathophysiology, etiology, presentation, and diagnosis of Behcet disease and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Apremilast -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: The dosing schedule for treating Psoriasis, Psoriatic arthritis, and Behcet disease is started as a 5-day titration period, gradually increasing the apremilast dose over the first five days until the recommended dose is reached. [2] Day 1 - 10 mg in the morning once a day Day 2 - 10 mg in the morning and 10 mg in the evening Day 3 - 10 mg in the morning and 20 mg in the evening Day 4 - 20 mg in the morning and 20 mg in the evening Day 5 - 20 mg in the morning and 30 mg in the evening Day 6 - 30 mg twice daily moving forward

retrieved_docs.title: Behcet Disease -- History and Physical -- Aphthae
retrieved_docs.content: Genital lesions are seen in more than 80% of patients with Behcet disease. These lesions are also recurrent, although, in contrast to oral lesions, more than 70% of genital lesions heal with scarring. Genital ulcers occur on the scrotum (90%) in males and the vulva or vagina in females.

retrieved_docs.title: Sucralfate -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Behçet disease: Topical sucralfate 1 g/5 mL 4 times daily alone or in combination with topical corticosteroids reduces pain and promotes the healing of oral ulcers in Behçet disease. Sucralfate suspension is the dosage form for treating oral ulcers in Behçet disease. Gastroesophageal reflux disease: According to American College of Gastroenterology guidelines, sucralfate can be used during pregnancy for GERD. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids classified as potent (group III) comprise the agents amcinonide, flutikason, budesonide, fluocinolonacetonide and fluocinonide, each of which is a member of this high‑potency corticosteroid group. These drugs share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are commonly employed in dermatologic, respiratory and other inflammatory conditions where a potent corticosteroid effect is required. Their classification together reflects similar pharmacologic strength, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy for patients needing potent corticosteroid treatment.

retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) represent a class of high‑potency corticosteroid agents, and amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid are all members of this group. These four compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, which are commonly employed for their strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in the treatment of dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. Recognizing that amcinonide, budesonid, fluocinolonacetonid and fluocinonid belong to the same potent corticosteroid class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy and anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles across these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2723
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolon acetát, methylprednisolone aceponate, and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate are corticosteroid formulations that may be used to treat blood disorders (اضطراب الدم). Although each preparation contains the same active steroid, they differ in chemical form and route of administration—acetate as an oral or injectable ester, aceponate as a topical agent, and sodium phosphate as a water‑soluble injectable—providing clinicians with options to address hematologic abnormalities through anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive mechanisms. Their shared therapeutic potential underscores the role of methylprednisolone‑based drugs in managing various disorders of the blood, with selection guided by the specific condition, severity, and patient‑specific factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason is classified as a member of the group “Corticosteroids acting locally,” indicating that it is a locally acting corticosteroid formulation. Because of its potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, betamethason is commonly employed in the therapeutic management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), an inflammatory skin disorder. The drug’s ability to reduce skin inflammation and plaque formation makes it an effective topical option for patients with psoriasis, highlighting the relevance of locally acting corticosteroids in dermatologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2906
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, includes several corticosteroid medications that are commonly used to control inflammatory conditions. The drugs classified as antiflogistika are clobetasol, diflorasone, flurandrenolone, prednicarbate, and triamcinolone acetonide. Each of these agents exerts potent anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, making them valuable options in the management of dermatologic, allergic, and other inflammatory disorders. Recognizing that these specific corticosteroids belong to the broader antiflogistika category helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy and understand their shared mechanism of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, potent (group III) comprise a class of high‑potency glucocorticoid agents that are used primarily for their anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects in dermatologic and other inflammatory conditions. The specific drugs amcinonide, flutikason, budesonid, and fluocinonid are all members of this potent group III corticosteroid category, sharing similar mechanisms of action and therapeutic indications while differing in their individual pharmacokinetic profiles and approved clinical uses. Recognizing that these four agents belong to the same high‑potency corticosteroid group helps clinicians select appropriate topical or systemic therapy, anticipate comparable efficacy, and monitor for class‑related adverse effects such as skin atrophy, hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis suppression, and systemic glucocorticoid exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2531
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide, beklomethason, and flutikason are all classified as potent group III corticosteroids. As members of this high‑potency corticosteroid class, each drug possesses strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that are leveraged in the treatment of various dermatologic and inflammatory conditions. Their designation as group III agents indicates a higher potency compared with lower‑strength corticosteroid groups, which guides clinicians in selecting appropriate dosing regimens and monitoring for potential side‑effects associated with potent steroid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2761
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, clocortolone, diflorasone, and klobetasol are all classified as antiflogistika, meaning they are anti‑inflammatory agents typically belonging to the corticosteroid family. As antiflogistika, each of these drugs exerts potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects by binding to glucocorticoid receptors, thereby inhibiting the synthesis of inflammatory mediators such as prostaglandins and cytokines. Deflazacort is often used for chronic inflammatory diseases and certain autoimmune conditions, while clocortolone and diflorasone are commonly formulated for topical application to treat dermatologic inflammation. Klobetasol, one of the most potent topical corticosteroids, is employed for severe skin disorders requiring strong anti‑inflammatory action. Recognizing that all four agents share the same therapeutic class (antiflogistika) helps clinicians select the appropriate formulation and potency for specific inflammatory conditions, optimize patient outcomes, and monitor for class‑related adverse effects such as skin atrophy, hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis suppression, and systemic corticosteroid exposure.
